notes.anchor: On the sixth day before the Kalends of February
notes.note: By the Roman inclusive count backward from the Kalends (1st) of February, the sixth day before is 27 January; the eighth day before, mentioned just after, is 25 January.

notes.anchor: Lucius Caesar brought Caesar's terms to Pompey
notes.note: L. Iulius Caesar the younger, a kinsman of the dictator, served as Caesar's envoy in January 49 BC, carrying his peace terms to Pompey and the consuls; he is distinct from his father, the consul of 64 BC, and from Caesar himself.

notes.anchor: while he was with the consuls at Teanum
notes.note: Caesar's offer was that he withdraw garrisons from towns seized outside his province (Cisalpine Gaul) in exchange for the Pompeians returning to Rome and settling matters through the senate; the consuls of 49 were with Pompey at Teanum Sidicinum to receive it.

notes.anchor: the colonists of Campania are responding with no great readiness
notes.note: The Campani coloni were settlers planted on the rich ager Campanus under Caesar's land law of 59 BC; unsurprisingly they showed little zeal for the levy raised against their benefactor.

Caesar owned a large gladiatorial training school (ludus) at Capua; fearing the gladiators might be turned loose, Pompey broke up the troupe by billeting them two at a time on respectable householders.
Atticus's sister Pomponia was the wife of Cicero's brother Quintus; she was among the women of the family whom Cicero urged to leave Rome as people of their rank were departing the city.
